key_points: Marginalisation refers to the process in which certain groups are pushed to the margins of society and denied equal opportunities
Marginalised communities often face social discrimination, economic inequality and limited access to resources
In India, groups such as Adivasis and some religious minorities experience marginalisation
Adivasis traditionally depend on forests and natural resources for their livelihood
Development projects, industrialisation and displacement have affected many Adivasi communities
Marginalisation can limit access to education, employment and political participation
The Constitution of India guarantees equality and provides protection for minority rights
examples: Many Adivasi communities were displaced from forests due to mining and industrial projects
Some minority groups face difficulties in accessing education and employment opportunities
Government welfare schemes aim to improve living conditions for marginalised communities
Social awareness movements work to protect the rights of disadvantaged groups
formulas: Marginalisation → Social exclusion + Discrimination + Limited opportunities
Social inclusion → Equality + Rights + Participation in society
Protection of marginalised groups → Constitution + Laws + Government policies
exam_tips: Explain the meaning of marginalisation with examples
Mention Adivasis and minorities when discussing marginalised communities
Write about the effects of marginalisation on education and employment
Explain how the Constitution and government policies help reduce marginalisation
